Justice Abhay Shreeniwas Oka is a judge of the Supreme Court of India who was elevated from the Karnataka High Court, where he served as Chief Justice. He is widely regarded as one of the most liberal and rights-conscious judges on the Supreme Court bench. Justice Oka is known for his strong positions on personal liberty, the right to bail, and the importance of the criminal justice system not being used as a tool of oppression. He has authored important judgments on habeas corpus and preventive detention.

Justice Bhushan Ramkrishna Gavai is a judge of the Supreme Court of India who is set to become the Chief Justice of India in 2025. He started his legal career as an advocate at the Nagpur Bar and was later elevated to the Bombay High Court. He is the second judge from the Scheduled Caste community to be appointed to the Supreme Court and is known for his strong commitment to constitutional values, social justice, and environmental protection.

Justice Dhananjaya Yeshwant Chandrachud served as the 50th Chief Justice of India from November 2022 to November 2024. He is regarded as one of the most impactful Chief Justices in recent history, having authored landmark judgments on privacy, constitutional morality, and judicial process. Before becoming CJI, he authored the majority opinion in the Puttaswamy (Privacy) case. He is known for his progressive judicial philosophy and for championing technology in the justice system, including the e-courts initiative.

Justice Hrishikesh Roy was elevated to the Supreme Court of India in July 2019 after serving as the Chief Justice of the Kerala High Court. He previously served as a judge of the Gauhati High Court and as an advocate in Assam. He is known for his work in areas of arbitration, constitutional law, and environmental law. Justice Roy has authored notable judgments on forest rights, environmental protection, and personal liberty.

Justice Jamshed Burjor Pardiwala is a judge of the Supreme Court of India elevated from the Gujarat High Court. He has expertise in constitutional law, criminal law, and corporate law. He is known for his incisive questioning during oral arguments and his clearly written judgments. Justice Pardiwala has authored significant judgments in areas including demonetisation, NEET paper leak, and criminal procedure reform.
Former Judge, Supreme Court of India

Justice Kurian Mammen Joseph served as a judge of the Supreme Court of India from 2018 to 2023. His appointment was initially controversial when the government rejected the collegium's recommendation before finally accepting it after re-recommendation. He served as Chief Justice of the Uttarakhand High Court before his elevation. Justice Joseph is known for his strong positions on judicial independence, federalism, and personal liberty. His retirement speech was widely noted for its candid remarks on the state of the judiciary.

Justice Manoj Misra is a judge of the Supreme Court of India who was elevated from the Allahabad High Court. Prior to his elevation to the high court, he was an advocate practising at the Allahabad High Court. He has expertise in constitutional law, criminal law, and revenue matters. Justice Misra has been part of benches hearing several significant cases including the Electoral Bonds case and the NEET paper leak case.

Justice Nuthalapati Venkata Ramana served as the 48th Chief Justice of India from April 2021 to August 2022. During his tenure, he focused on addressing the backlog of cases, filling judicial vacancies, and strengthening the collegium system. He authored important judgments on the right to be forgotten, free speech, and the independence of the judiciary. Justice Ramana began his legal career as an advocate practising in the Andhra Pradesh High Court before being elevated to the bench.

Justice Pamidighantam Sri Narasimha is a judge of the Supreme Court of India who was directly elevated from the Bar — a relatively rare occurrence. He served as Additional Solicitor General of India and Senior Advocate before his appointment. He is known for his expertise in constitutional law, arbitration, and commercial law. Justice Narasimha has been part of important Constitution Bench hearings on electoral bonds, same-sex marriage, and citizenship.

Justice Sanjiv Khanna was appointed as the 51st Chief Justice of India on November 11, 2024. He has been a judge of the Supreme Court since January 2019 and is known for his meticulous approach to constitutional and commercial law. He is a graduate of the Delhi University Faculty of Law and practised at the Delhi High Court before his elevation to the bench. Justice Khanna has authored several landmark judgments in areas including electoral law, tax, and fundamental rights.
Senior Judge, Supreme Court of India

Justice Surya Kant is one of the senior-most judges of the Supreme Court of India. Prior to his elevation, he served as the Chief Justice of the Himachal Pradesh High Court and as a judge of the Punjab and Haryana High Court. He is known for his expertise in constitutional law, criminal law, and service matters. Justice Surya Kant has been part of several Constitution Benches and is known for his plain-spoken judgments and concern for access to justice.

Justice Uday Umesh Lalit served as the 49th Chief Justice of India for a brief tenure from August to November 2022. He was the second judge to be directly elevated from the Bar to the Supreme Court. Known for his expertise in criminal law and his efficient court management, he initiated several reforms during his short tenure as CJI including constituting five-judge Constitution Benches on important pending matters. He retired after 74 days as Chief Justice.

A five-judge Constitution Bench upheld the Union government's August 2019 decision to abrogate Article 370, which had granted special status to Jammu and Kashmir. The court held the abrogation was constitutionally valid and also upheld the bifurcation of the state into two Union Territories. The bench directed that statehood be restored to Jammu and Kashmir at the earliest and that elections be held by September 2024.

## Background

On August 5, 2019, the Union government issued a Presidential Order under Article 370, effectively abrogating the provision and stripping Jammu and Kashmir of its special status. The state was simultaneously bifurcated into two Union Territories — Jammu and Kashmir (with a legislature) and Ladakh (without a legislature). Multiple petitions were filed challenging these decisions.

## Constitutional Questions

The bench was tasked with determining:

1. Whether Article 370 was a temporary or permanent provision of the Constitution.
2. Whether the President had the power to abrogate Article 370 unilaterally.
3. Whether the bifurcation of a state without the consent of its legislature was constitutionally permissible.
4. Whether the imposition of President's Rule was a condition precedent for the abrogation.

## The Judgment

The five-judge bench, in a unanimous verdict, upheld the abrogation. Chief Justice D.Y. Chandrachud held that Article 370 was indeed a temporary provision that could be abrogated by the President. The court also upheld the bifurcation while directing the restoration of statehood to Jammu and Kashmir and the holding of elections.

## Significance

The judgment settled a decade-long constitutional question about the status of Article 370 and the sovereignty of the Indian Union over Jammu and Kashmir. Critics, however, argued that the court's reasoning bypassed the federal character of the Constitution by allowing the Centre to convert a state into Union Territories.

Bulldozer Demolitions of Alleged Offenders' Properties

The Supreme Court issued comprehensive guidelines prohibiting state governments from demolishing the properties of accused persons without following due process. The court held that 'bulldozer justice' — the practice of demolishing homes of alleged offenders as a punitive measure — violates the rule of law, the right to shelter, and the principles of natural justice. Demolitions can only be carried out after issuing proper notice and following statutory procedures.

## Background

Beginning around 2020, several state governments — particularly Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and Rajasthan — began demolishing properties of individuals accused of criminal offences or communal violence. These demolitions, dubbed "bulldozer justice" by the media, were presented by governments as enforcement of building regulations, but critics argued they were extrajudicial punishments targeting specific communities.

## Supreme Court's Intervention

The Supreme Court took suo motu cognisance of the matter and also admitted petitions challenging the demolitions. The court expressed alarm at the "alarming trend" of executive action bypassing judicial processes.

## Guidelines Issued

In its November 2024 judgment, the court issued comprehensive guidelines:

1. **Prior notice**: A minimum of 15 days' notice must be given before any demolition.
2. **Hearing**: The affected person must be given an opportunity to be heard.
3. **Response time**: The person must be given time to challenge the demolition order.
4. **Written orders**: Demolition orders must be in writing with reasons.
5. **No collective punishment**: The property of an entire family cannot be demolished for the alleged crime of one member.
6. **Independent of criminal process**: Demolitions cannot be linked to criminal accusations; they must be based solely on violations of building regulations.

## Significance

The judgment is a landmark ruling on the right to housing and due process. The court emphasised that in a constitutional democracy, the state cannot act as judge, jury, and executioner. The guidelines set important guardrails against arbitrary executive action.

Over 200 petitions challenge the constitutional validity of the Citizenship Amendment Act, 2019, which fast-tracks citizenship for persecuted minorities from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan but excludes Muslims. Petitioners argue the Act violates Article 14 (right to equality) and Article 25 (right to religion) by creating an unconstitutional religion-based classification. The case raises fundamental questions about the secular character of the Indian Constitution.

## Background

The Citizenship Amendment Act, 2019 (CAA) was passed by Parliament on December 11, 2019 and received Presidential assent on December 12, 2019. It amends the Citizenship Act, 1955 to provide an expedited pathway to Indian citizenship for members of Hindu, Sikh, Buddhist, Jain, Parsi, and Christian communities who fled religious persecution from Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Afghanistan before December 31, 2014.

## Constitutional Challenge

The petitioners, including the Indian Union Muslim League, the Kerala government, and various civil society organisations, challenge the Act on multiple grounds:

- **Article 14**: The Act creates an arbitrary classification based on religion that violates the right to equality.
- **Secular character**: The exclusion of Muslims from the Act strikes at the secular character of the Constitution.
- **Article 21**: The Act, combined with the proposed National Register of Citizens, could render Muslims stateless.

## Government's Position

The Union of India argues that the classification is based on a reasonable nexus to the object of the legislation — providing refuge to persecuted minorities who cannot seek protection from the governments of their own countries. The government contends that since the three named countries are Islamic republics, Muslims cannot be considered persecuted minorities.

## Current Status

The matter has been admitted and is pending before a five-judge Constitution Bench. Arguments have commenced but a final hearing date is yet to be fixed.

Definition of Industry Under the Industrial Disputes Act
Workmen of the Bangalore Water Supply

A Constitution Bench of the Supreme Court is re-examining the definition of 'industry' under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947. The court is reconsidering its earlier landmark judgment in Bangalore Water Supply case to determine which entities fall within the protective ambit of industrial dispute legislation. This case has significant implications for labour rights across a wide range of institutions including hospitals, educational bodies, and charitable organisations.

## Background

The definition of "industry" under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947 has been a matter of significant legal debate since the landmark Bangalore Water Supply and Sewerage Board v. A. Rajappa (1978) judgment. In that case, a seven-judge Constitution Bench had laid down an expansive definition that brought within the Act's purview a wide range of activities.

## Legal Issues

The primary question before the current Constitution Bench is whether the expansive definition laid down in Bangalore Water Supply continues to hold good, or whether it needs to be revisited in light of changed economic and social conditions. The court must also determine:

1. Whether sovereign and regal functions of the state can be brought within the definition of industry.
2. Whether institutions primarily engaged in charitable or religious activities qualify as industries.
3. How the 2010 amendment to the Industrial Disputes Act affects the scope of the definition.

## Current Status

The Constitution Bench has heard several rounds of arguments from both management and union sides. The matter has been referred to a larger bench due to conflicting precedents and is scheduled for further hearing in May 2026.

A five-judge Constitution Bench upheld the Union government's November 2016 decision to demonetise Rs 500 and Rs 1000 currency notes by a 4:1 majority. The majority held that the decision satisfied the test of proportionality and that the government had legitimate aims in combating black money, terrorism financing, and counterfeit currency. Justice B.V. Nagarathna, in a notable dissent, held the demonetisation to be legally flawed as it was initiated by the executive without adequate parliamentary sanction.

## Background

On November 8, 2016, Prime Minister Narendra Modi announced that Rs 500 and Rs 1000 currency notes would cease to be legal tender from midnight that day. This demonetisation exercise, which invalidated approximately 86% of India's currency in circulation, was challenged through over 50 petitions in the Supreme Court.

## Legal Issues

The petitioners raised several constitutional and statutory challenges:

1. Whether the government had the power to demonetise currency notes under Section 26(2) of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.
2. Whether the decision was taken in proper consultation with the RBI.
3. Whether the notification was arbitrary and violated Articles 14, 19, and 21.
4. Whether the short window for exchange of old currency was proportionate.

## The Judgment

The four-judge majority upheld the demonetisation, finding that:
- The government had the power to demonetise under the RBI Act.
- The decision was taken after proper consultation with the RBI Board.
- The measures were proportionate to the legitimate aims of curbing black money.

## Justice Nagarathna's Dissent

In a significant dissent, Justice B.V. Nagarathna held that demonetisation of such a large proportion of currency could only have been done by Parliament through legislation, not by the executive through a notification. She found the process legally flawed, though she acknowledged the laudable aims of the exercise.

## Legacy

The 6-year delay between the demonetisation and the judgment was widely criticised. The dissent by Justice Nagarathna is considered by legal scholars to be the more legally sound position, raising important questions about parliamentary oversight of major economic decisions.

A five-judge Constitution Bench unanimously struck down the Electoral Bonds Scheme, 2018, holding it unconstitutional. The court found that the scheme violated citizens' right to information about political funding and enabled unlimited, anonymous corporate donations to political parties. The judgment directed the State Bank of India to stop issuing electoral bonds and to submit data on all bonds to the Election Commission of India.

## Background

The Electoral Bonds Scheme was introduced in 2018, allowing individuals and corporations to purchase bonds from the State Bank of India and donate them to political parties. The scheme promised anonymity to donors, with the SBI being the only entity that could match bonds to donors.

## The Judgment

On February 15, 2024, a unanimous five-judge Constitution Bench struck down the Electoral Bonds Scheme. Chief Justice D.Y. Chandrachud, writing for the court, held:

- **Right to Information**: Voters have a constitutionally protected right under Article 19(1)(a) to know who funds political parties, as this information is essential for making an informed electoral choice.
- **Disproportionate restriction**: The scheme's anonymity provisions were not proportionate to the stated aim of curbing black money in elections.
- **Unlimited corporate donations**: The removal of the earlier cap on corporate donations violated the principle of free and fair elections.

## Post-Judgment Developments

Following the judgment, the Election Commission published data on electoral bond purchases and encashments as directed. The data revealed significant correlations between large donations and subsequent government contracts or policy decisions, sparking a nationwide political controversy.

## Significance

The Electoral Bonds judgment is considered one of the most significant constitutional rulings in recent years. It reaffirmed the right to information as a cornerstone of democratic governance and set important limits on the role of money in electoral politics.

Karnataka Hijab Ban in Educational Institutions

The Supreme Court delivered a split verdict on the constitutional validity of Karnataka's ban on wearing hijab in pre-university colleges. Justice Hemant Gupta upheld the Karnataka government's order while Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia struck it down as unconstitutional and a violation of the right to education and dignity. Due to the split verdict, the matter was referred to a larger bench but has since remained undecided.

## Background

In February 2022, Muslim girl students in Karnataka were barred from attending pre-university colleges wearing hijab after the college development committees issued orders prescribing a uniform dress code. The Karnataka High Court upheld the ban in March 2022, holding that wearing the hijab was not an essential religious practice in Islam. The students appealed to the Supreme Court.

## The Split Verdict

The two-judge bench delivered diametrically opposite opinions:

**Justice Hemant Gupta** upheld the High Court's decision and found:
- Wearing hijab is not an essential religious practice.
- Educational institutions can impose reasonable dress codes.
- The government order was a reasonable restriction on the fundamental right to religion.

**Justice Sudhanshu Dhulia** struck down the ban and found:
- The issue should not have been examined through the lens of "essential religious practice."
- A student's right to wear hijab is part of her right to privacy, dignity, and education.
- The ban was an unnecessary intrusion that forced girls to choose between their faith and education.

## Referral to Larger Bench

Due to the split verdict, the matter was referred to a larger bench. As of the date of this report, the case has not been listed before a larger bench and the Karnataka government's order remains in force.

## Significance

The case raises profound questions about the scope of the "essential religious practice" doctrine and whether courts should be determining what constitutes a mandatory practice within a religion. Justice Dhulia's opinion is widely regarded by legal scholars as a more constitutionally sound approach rooted in individual autonomy.

National Testing Agency & Union of India

The Supreme Court declined to order a re-test of the NEET-UG 2024 examination following allegations of paper leaks, finding insufficient evidence of systemic compromise of the entire examination. However, the court expressed serious concern about the National Testing Agency's functioning and upheld the cancellation of results in specific centres where irregularities were established. The court also directed a high-level committee to review the NTA's processes.

## Background

In May 2024, following the NEET-UG examination, serious allegations emerged about paper leaks in multiple states. Students and organisations filed petitions in the Supreme Court seeking cancellation of the entire examination and a fresh re-test. The National Testing Agency (NTA), which conducts the exam, faced severe criticism for its handling of the situation.

## Court Proceedings

The Supreme Court heard the matter on an urgent basis. Petitioners argued that the scale of the paper leak was so large that the integrity of the entire examination was compromised, making a re-test essential. The government and NTA argued that the leak was localised to specific centres.

## The Judgment

The three-judge bench, in a detailed judgment, held:

- The petitioners had not established that the paper leak was so widespread as to have benefited candidates across the country.
- "Systemic breach" of examination integrity would require compelling evidence going beyond specific instances.
- The court cannot order a re-test — affecting 23 lakh students — based on speculation.
- However, results at specific centres where irregularities were proven were to be cancelled.

## Institutional Reforms

The court endorsed the formation of a high-level committee under former ISRO chairman K. Radhakrishnan to review the NTA's examination processes. The committee's recommendations led to significant reforms in how competitive examinations are conducted in India.

## Significance

The case highlighted the vulnerability of India's high-stakes examination system to corruption and the devastating consequences for millions of students. The judgment balanced individual rights against the practical impossibility of conducting a re-test while setting important standards for examination integrity.

The Supreme Court constituted a technical expert committee to investigate allegations that the Israeli Pegasus spyware was used to surveil Indian journalists, activists, and politicians. The court held that mere invocation of national security by the government does not give it a free pass to violate fundamental rights, and that surveillance must comply with constitutional standards. The committee's report was submitted under seal to the court.

## Background

In July 2021, an international consortium of journalists published the Pegasus Project — a report revealing that NSO Group's Pegasus spyware had been used to surveil journalists, human rights activists, politicians, and lawyers across the world. The report indicated that India was among the countries using the spyware, with targets including political opponents and journalists.

## Court's Intervention

Multiple petitions were filed in the Supreme Court seeking an independent investigation. Chief Justice N.V. Ramana, speaking for the bench, observed that "the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21" and that "if the allegations are true, it is a serious matter."

## Technical Committee

The court constituted a three-member technical expert committee, chaired by former Supreme Court judge R.V. Raveendran, to:
- Examine whether Pegasus spyware was used on Indian devices.
- Identify those whose phones were hacked.
- Recommend steps to prevent illegal surveillance.

## Outcome

The technical committee examined 29 phones submitted by petitioners and found malware on some devices but could not conclusively establish it was Pegasus. The Union government refused to cooperate with the committee citing national security. The committee's full findings were submitted to the court under seal in August 2022 and the case was closed.

## Legacy

The case established important principles: that national security cannot be a blanket justification for surveillance, and that any surveillance must comply with the requirements of Article 21. These principles continue to guide surveillance-related litigation.

A nine-judge Constitution Bench is examining fundamental questions about the relationship between religion, gender equality, and constitutional morality. The bench was constituted after review petitions were filed against the 2018 judgment allowing women of all ages to enter the Sabarimala temple. The court must now determine whether essential religious practices can override constitutional guarantees of gender equality.

## Background

In September 2018, a five-judge Constitutional Bench of the Supreme Court, by a 4:1 majority, held that the exclusion of women between the ages of 10 and 50 from the Sabarimala temple in Kerala was unconstitutional. The majority held that the practice violated Articles 14, 15, 17, and 25 of the Constitution.

## Review Petitions

Following the judgment, thousands of review petitions were filed. In November 2019, a five-judge bench, by a 3:2 majority, referred the matter to a larger nine-judge bench to settle overarching constitutional questions including:

1. What is the interplay between freedom of religion under Articles 25 and 26 and other fundamental rights?
2. Whether the right to equality and non-discrimination under Articles 14 and 15 override religious denominational rights?
3. Whether the "essential religious practices" doctrine needs to be revisited?

## Significance

The nine-judge bench's ruling will have far-reaching implications not just for Sabarimala but also for cases involving entry of Muslim women into mosques and Parsi women who marry outside the faith into fire temples. The court is essentially being asked to determine the limits of judicial review of religious customs.

## Current Status

Arguments before the nine-judge bench have been heard in stages. The bench is yet to deliver its judgment. Multiple amici curiae have been appointed to assist the court.

A five-judge Constitution Bench declined to grant constitutional recognition to same-sex marriages, holding that there is no fundamental right to marry under the Indian Constitution. The majority ruled that the court cannot read same-sex marriage into the Special Marriage Act and that any change in the law must come from Parliament. However, the court directed the Union government to constitute a committee to examine the rights of non-heterosexual couples.

## Background

Following the Supreme Court's 2018 judgment decriminalising homosexuality (Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India), LGBTQ+ couples and activists filed petitions seeking recognition of same-sex marriages. The petitions primarily sought amendments to the Special Marriage Act, 1954 to include same-sex couples.

## The Hearings

The ten-day hearing before the Constitution Bench attracted significant public interest. The bench heard arguments about whether the Constitution recognises a fundamental right to marry, whether non-heterosexual couples have equal rights under Articles 14 and 21, and whether the court could read gender-neutral provisions into the Special Marriage Act.

## The Judgment

The five-judge bench delivered a split verdict. Chief Justice Chandrachud and Justice Kaul (in a minority) held that LGBTQ+ couples have a right to form unions but not necessarily to marry. The majority (Justices Bhat, Kohli, and Narasimha) held that no such right exists and that the matter must be left to Parliament.

Key findings:
- There is no fundamental right to marry under the Indian Constitution.
- The court cannot rewrite legislation; Parliament must amend the law.
- Queer couples have certain rights including the right to adopt, though this too was a contested point.

## Criticism and Reception

The judgment was criticised by LGBTQ+ advocates as a missed opportunity, while others praised the court's restraint in not usurping the legislative function. Chief Justice Chandrachud's comments during the hearing about the "holistic package of rights" for queer couples were seen as promising but not fulfilled by the final order.

A Constitution Bench is examining the constitutional validity of Section 124A of the Indian Penal Code, which criminalises sedition. The court had previously ordered a stay on all sedition cases while the government indicated it would reconsider the law. With the IPC having been replaced by the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita in 2024, the challenge has partially shifted but Section 152 of the new code contains similar provisions that remain under scrutiny.

## Background

Section 124A of the Indian Penal Code, which makes sedition a criminal offence punishable with up to life imprisonment, was enacted by the British colonial government in 1870 primarily to suppress the Indian independence movement. It has been used extensively against journalists, activists, and government critics.

## Previous Supreme Court Orders

In May 2022, a three-judge bench headed by Chief Justice N.V. Ramana stayed all proceedings under Section 124A and directed the government to reconsider the law. The government had initially indicated willingness to review the provision, but subsequent administrations have been less forthcoming.

## New Criminal Laws

The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (which replaced the IPC from July 1, 2024) does not contain a provision specifically labelled "sedition" but includes Section 152, which criminalises acts endangering the "sovereignty, unity and integrity of India." Critics argue that Section 152 is essentially the old sedition law with new language.

## Current Legal Position

The constitutional challenge now encompasses both the old Section 124A and the new Section 152. The petitioners argue that any law criminalising criticism of the government without requiring incitement to violence violates Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech). The government argues that such laws are necessary for national security.

## Significance

A ruling on this case will significantly impact the legal landscape for freedom of speech in India and determine the extent to which criticism of government policies can be criminalised.

Bulldozer Justice and the Right to Housing

The Supreme Court's comprehensive guidelines against extrajudicial demolitions represent a landmark affirmation of the right to housing and the rule of law. This analysis examines the court's reasoning and the challenges that remain in implementation.

## The Phenomenon of Bulldozer Justice

The term "bulldozer justice" entered India's political vocabulary around 2020, when the Uttar Pradesh government began demolishing the homes and businesses of individuals accused of serious crimes, particularly those related to communal violence. The governments of Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, and other BJP-ruled states followed suit. Proponents argued it was a show of strength against criminals; opponents argued it was extrajudicial punishment targeted primarily at the Muslim community.

The Supreme Court's engagement with this practice was initially cautious. The court repeatedly stayed specific demolitions but hesitated to issue broader guidelines. In November 2024, a two-judge bench of Justices B.R. Gavai and K.V. Viswanathan finally issued comprehensive guidelines after hearing the matter over several hearings.

## The Constitutional Framework

The court's guidelines rest on several constitutional foundations. First, Article 21 protects the right to shelter as a component of the right to life. Second, the principles of natural justice — that no one can be condemned without a hearing — apply to demolitions just as they do to any adverse state action. Third, the doctrine of proportionality requires that even legitimate regulatory action (like demolishing an illegal structure) must be proportionate and must not be used as a pretext for punitive action.

## What the Guidelines Say

The court's guidelines are detailed and operational. They require prior notice of at least 15 days, an opportunity to be heard, a written order with reasons, and adequate time to approach higher courts for relief. Critically, the guidelines prohibit any linkage between a demolition order and a criminal accusation — a demolition must be based solely on a violation of building regulations, not on the fact that the occupant is an accused.

## The Implementation Challenge

The most significant challenge is ensuring that guidelines issued by the Supreme Court translate into actual change on the ground. State governments have historically been reluctant to abandon the political utility of demolitions, which generate images of decisive action against criminals. The court has indicated it will monitor compliance through contempt proceedings, but the practical effectiveness of this mechanism remains to be tested.

Cutting the Wire: The Supreme Court on ISP Liability

A recent Supreme Court order has significant implications for how internet service providers are treated under Indian law. This analysis examines the court's reasoning and what it means for digital rights and corporate liability.

## The Question of ISP Liability

The Supreme Court's recent engagement with the question of internet service provider liability marks a turning point in India's evolving digital jurisprudence. The court's order, delivered in a batch of petitions concerning website blocking directives under the Information Technology Act, 2000, has significant implications for how ISPs navigate their dual obligations — to comply with government orders on one hand and to protect the rights of their users on the other.

The petitioners in the case argued that ISPs are being made unwitting instruments of censorship, required to block entire websites or platforms based on administrative orders without any judicial oversight. This, they contended, violates the principles laid down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015), where the court struck down Section 66A of the IT Act and read down the blocking provisions of Section 69A to require procedural safeguards.

## Proportionality and Collateral Damage

A central theme in the court's analysis was the principle of proportionality. When an ISP blocks a website URL to comply with a government order, it may inadvertently block thousands of other URLs hosted on the same IP address or domain. This "over-blocking" or collateral damage to free speech is a well-documented phenomenon that courts around the world have struggled to address.

The court observed that the blocking rules under Section 69A must be read in light of the constitutional guarantee of free speech under Article 19(1)(a). Any restriction, including blocking of content, must satisfy the tests of legality, necessity, and proportionality. A blanket block that affects unrelated content cannot be justified merely because some content on a platform is deemed objectionable.

## Implications for Digital Rights

This judgment has significant implications beyond ISP liability. It signals that the Supreme Court is willing to apply rigorous constitutional scrutiny to government actions in the digital sphere. It reinforces the principle that the internet cannot be governed through the same broad discretionary powers that might be permissible in other contexts. The challenge now lies in translating these principles into consistent practice across the hundreds of blocking orders issued every year.

Electoral Bonds: What the Court Struck Down and Why
A data-driven examination of the Supreme Court's landmark Electoral Bonds judgment — what the scheme was, who bought bonds, who received them, and why the court found it unconstitutional.

## What Were Electoral Bonds?

Electoral bonds were bearer instruments introduced by the Finance Act, 2017 and operationalised through the Electoral Bond Scheme, 2018. They were available in denominations from Rs 1,000 to Rs 1 crore, could be purchased by any citizen or company, and could be donated to any registered political party. The scheme's key feature was anonymity — while the State Bank of India knew who had purchased bonds, the Election Commission and the public did not.

## The Numbers

Between March 2018 and February 2024, electoral bonds worth approximately Rs 16,518 crore were purchased. A disproportionate share — nearly 55% — went to the ruling Bharatiya Janata Party. The opposition Indian National Congress received approximately 10%. Data released pursuant to the Supreme Court's order revealed that several large corporate donors had received significant government contracts, regulatory approvals, or policy benefits around the time of their donations.

## Why the Court Struck it Down

The Constitution Bench unanimously found the scheme unconstitutional on two main grounds:

**Right to Information**: The court held that voters have a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a) to know who funds political parties. This information is essential for voters to make an informed choice at the ballot box. Anonymity in political funding is thus not a mere procedural matter but one that goes to the heart of democratic accountability.

**Proportionality**: The court applied the proportionality test from its privacy jurisprudence and found that even if protecting donor privacy had some legitimacy, making donations completely anonymous was not a proportionate means of achieving that end. Less restrictive alternatives existed, such as requiring disclosure above a threshold amount.

## Aftermath and Continuing Questions

The Election Commission's disclosure of bond data triggered significant political controversy and investigative journalism. The data revealed patterns suggesting a possible quid pro quo between corporate donations and regulatory or policy outcomes, though no court has yet definitively established such a connection. The question of how to fund political parties in a transparent manner without exposing donors to victimisation remains unresolved.

Judicial Vacancies in the Supreme Court: A Data Story

A comprehensive data analysis of judicial vacancies in the Supreme Court and high courts of India, examining trends in appointments, retirements, and the persistent gap between sanctioned and working strength.

## The Numbers at a Glance

As of January 2025, the Supreme Court of India has 33 judges against a sanctioned strength of 34. While near full strength at the apex court, the situation at the high courts tells a different story: across India's 25 high courts, the combined working strength is approximately 770 judges against a sanctioned strength of 1,122 — a vacancy rate of nearly 31%.

This means that more than one in three judicial posts in the high courts is unfilled, creating cascading effects on the pace of justice delivery. Cases take longer to be decided, bail applications take longer to be heard, and urgent matters are routinely delayed for want of judicial time.

## Trends in Appointments

Data from the Supreme Court Collegium's recommendations over the past five years reveals several patterns. First, the gap between recommendation and appointment has widened — what used to take weeks now routinely takes months or years. Second, the government's rate of returning collegium recommendations has increased, even after the 99th Constitutional Amendment and NJAC Act were struck down in 2015. Third, several high courts — particularly those in smaller states — consistently operate at below 60% of their sanctioned strength.

## The Collegium System Under Scrutiny

The collegium system, which gives the Chief Justice and senior judges of the Supreme Court the primary say in judicial appointments, has faced criticism from multiple directions. Critics on the right argue it creates a self-perpetuating oligarchy insulated from democratic accountability. Critics on the left argue that the government's influence over the collegium's choices distorts the pool of candidates. Data shows that the diversity of the judiciary — in terms of gender, region, and social background — remains limited, though there has been modest improvement in recent years.

## What the Data Does Not Capture

Behind the statistics are individual stories: the first-generation litigant in Bihar who has been waiting eight years for her property dispute to be resolved; the undertrial prisoner in Maharashtra who has been in jail for three years awaiting trial on charges that carry a maximum sentence of two years; the small business owner in Tamil Nadu whose insolvency petition has been pending for four years while his business deteriorates. Judicial vacancies are not an administrative problem — they are a human rights crisis, playing out one delayed hearing at a time.

After the NEET Verdict: What Changed?

The Supreme Court's decision not to order a NEET re-test was controversial, but the broader systemic changes it triggered may prove to be its lasting legacy. Six months after the verdict, we examine what has and has not changed in India's examination ecosystem.

## The Immediate Aftermath

The Supreme Court's August 2024 order declining a wholesale NEET re-test was met with mixed reactions. Students who had not been implicated in any irregularity were relieved; those in affected centres felt let down. The National Testing Agency, however, faced sustained institutional pressure. Within weeks of the judgment, the government replaced the NTA director general and announced a comprehensive review of the agency's operations.

## The Radhakrishnan Committee Recommendations

The high-level committee chaired by former ISRO chairman K. Radhakrishnan submitted its report in September 2024. Its key recommendations included: decentralisation of examination centres, end-to-end digital encryption of question papers, biometric verification of candidates, and a complete overhaul of the NTA's governance structure. The government accepted most recommendations, though the timeline for implementation was criticised as too slow.

## Structural Changes to the NTA

The NTA's restructuring has involved separating its examination and administrative functions, creating an independent integrity unit, and establishing real-time monitoring of all examination processes. The agency has also piloted computer-based testing for a larger proportion of its examinations, reducing the vulnerability to physical paper leaks. Whether these changes will be sufficient to prevent future leaks remains to be seen.

## What Has Not Changed

Despite the systemic changes, the fundamental problem — high-stakes examinations determining access to scarce seats in elite institutions — remains intact. The enormous financial rewards for successful cheating continue to incentivise corruption. The coaching industry, which turns examination preparation into an all-consuming commercial enterprise, continues to flourish. Students from disadvantaged backgrounds continue to face structural barriers that go far beyond examination integrity. The NEET verdict may have addressed some symptoms but has not touched the underlying pathologies of India's examination culture.

Who Counts as a Scheduled Caste Person After Religious Conversion?

A seven-judge Constitution Bench is examining whether Dalits who convert to Christianity or Islam can continue to claim Scheduled Caste status. This analysis traces the legal history of caste and religion in India and what the court's ruling might mean for millions of people.

## The Presidential Orders and their Religious Restriction

At the heart of the case lies the Constitution (Scheduled Castes) Order, 1950, as amended over the years, which determines who qualifies as a Scheduled Caste for the purposes of reservations in education, employment, and political representation. The Order originally restricted SC status to Hindus, was later expanded to include Sikhs (1956) and Buddhists (1990), but continues to exclude Christians and Muslims.

The exclusion rests on the assumption that caste discrimination is intrinsic to Hinduism (and by extension, some dharmic religions) but does not exist, or exists in a different form, within egalitarian Abrahamic faiths. Petitioners challenging this exclusion argue that caste is a social reality that persists regardless of religion — a Dalit Christian or Dalit Muslim continues to face the same social and economic discrimination as a Dalit Hindu.

## The Evidence on Caste Discrimination After Conversion

The National Commission for Religious and Linguistic Minorities (the Ranganath Misra Commission) and the Justice Rajinder Sachar Committee both documented the persistence of caste discrimination among converted communities. Dalit Christians and Dalit Muslims, despite having converted generations ago, continue to face social ostracism, economic exclusion, and untouchability in practice. These findings form a critical part of the petitioners' case.

The government's opposing argument draws a distinction between social discrimination and legal disability. It argues that Scheduled Caste status was designed to address the specific form of discrimination rooted in the Hindu caste system's ritual hierarchy, and that extending it to converts from other religions would dilute its remedial purpose.

## What the Court Decides Will Affect Millions

The Constitution Bench's ruling will have immediate and practical consequences for an estimated 20-30 million Dalit Christians and Dalit Muslims in India. A ruling in favour of the petitioners could make them eligible for reservations and other affirmative action benefits that have been denied to their communities for over seven decades. A ruling against could entrench a distinction that has been criticised as both legally tenuous and socially harmful. The stakes could not be higher.

Courts may invite amici curiae (plural) when they require assistance on complex legal or factual questions, or when the affected population is not otherwise represented in the proceedings.
From Latin meaning 'friend of the court' — someone who assists the court without being a party to the dispute.

The Supreme Court appoints a senior advocate as amicus curiae in a complex public interest case involving the rights of tribal communities, giving them an independent voice before the court without favouring either the petitioner or respondent.

## Role of Amicus Curiae in Indian Courts

The amicus curiae plays a particularly important role in Indian constitutional courts, especially in cases involving constitutional interpretation, PIL matters, and situations where vulnerable populations are affected. In the Sabarimala Review case, several senior advocates were appointed as amici to assist the nine-judge bench. In the same-sex marriage case, the court appointed amici to present arguments not covered by the parties.

## Distinction from Intervenors

An amicus curiae differs from an intervenor, who is a person or organisation that has a sufficient interest in the proceedings to be joined as a party. An amicus presents arguments to assist the court but does not advocate for any particular outcome in the same way as a party. However, in practice, the distinction can sometimes blur when amici are appointed to represent the interests of a particular constituency.

## Court-Appointed vs. Applied-For

In India, the court typically appoints amici in complex matters of public importance on its own initiative. However, individuals and organisations can also apply to be heard as amicus curiae, and the court exercises discretion in accepting such applications. The appointment of a well-regarded senior advocate as amicus can significantly shape the direction of judicial discourse in a case.

A writ by which a higher court reviews the decision of a lower court or tribunal. The writ is issued to quash an order that is either without jurisdiction, in excess of jurisdiction, or vitiated by an error of law apparent on the face of the record.
From Latin meaning 'to be certified' or 'to be made certain' — indicating the superior court's review of the lower court's record.

A party aggrieved by a quasi-judicial order of a statutory tribunal files a certiorari petition before the High Court to quash the order on the ground that the tribunal ignored relevant evidence and violated principles of natural justice.

## Scope of Certiorari in India

The scope of certiorari under Article 226 of the Indian Constitution is wider than its counterpart in English law. Indian courts have expanded the writ to include errors of law on the face of the record, violations of natural justice, and decisions made without considering relevant material. The distinction between a mere error of law and an error going to jurisdiction has been progressively narrowed.

## Certiorari and Natural Justice

One of the most significant grounds for certiorari in India is the violation of natural justice — specifically the principles of audi alteram partem (hear the other side) and nemo judex in causa sua (no one should be a judge in their own cause). An order passed without giving the affected party an opportunity to be heard is generally amenable to being quashed by certiorari.

A writ requiring a person under arrest to be brought before a judge or into court, especially to secure the person's release unless lawful grounds are shown for their detention. It is one of the great writs and a fundamental safeguard of individual liberty against arbitrary state action.
From Latin meaning 'you shall have the body' — referring to the requirement that the detaining authority produce the detained person before the court.
A family member files a habeas corpus petition when their relative is detained by the police without producing them before a magistrate within 24 hours as required by Article 22 of the Constitution.

## In Indian Constitutional Law

Under Article 32 of the Indian Constitution, the Supreme Court has the power to issue writs including habeas corpus for enforcement of fundamental rights. Under Article 226, High Courts have similar powers with a wider scope. The writ of habeas corpus is considered a bulwark against illegal detention and is one of the most important tools for protecting personal liberty.

## Historical Significance in India

The Emergency period of 1975-77 saw the most dramatic curtailment of habeas corpus rights in independent India. In the infamous ADM Jabalpur case (1976), the Supreme Court, by a 4:1 majority, held that during Emergency, the right to approach the court through habeas corpus could be suspended. This decision was later described by the court itself as "seriously erroneous" in the 2017 Puttaswamy (Privacy) judgment. The lone dissent by Justice H.R. Khanna became legendary and is considered one of the finest moments of judicial courage in Indian legal history.

## Grounds for Habeas Corpus

A habeas corpus petition can be filed on various grounds including: detention without adequate legal authority, detention in violation of constitutional safeguards, detention by a private person without authority of law, and failure to produce before a magistrate within the statutory time period.

The legal capacity or right of a person or organisation to bring an action in a court of law. A party must demonstrate sufficient connection to and harm from a law or action before they have the standing to challenge it. Also known as 'standing to sue'.
From Latin meaning 'place of standing' — referring to the petitioner's recognised position before the court.

A trade union files a public interest litigation before the Supreme Court on behalf of unorganised workers, arguing that the union has locus standi to represent their interests even though no individual worker has filed a petition.

## Traditional Locus Standi in India

Under the traditional common law rule, only a person who has suffered a personal injury or infringement of their own legal rights could file a petition in court. This requirement was strictly enforced by Indian courts in the decades following independence, limiting access to justice to those who could afford legal representation and who had clearly established personal interests.

## The PIL Revolution and Expanded Standing

The concept of locus standi was fundamentally transformed in India through the public interest litigation (PIL) jurisprudence developed by Justices P.N. Bhagwati and V.R. Krishna Iyer in the late 1970s and 1980s. The court held that any public-spirited individual could file a petition on behalf of those who were unable to access justice themselves — prisoners, bonded labourers, slum dwellers, and others on the margins of society. This expanded standing has been described as one of the most significant procedural innovations in Indian legal history.

## Misuse and Judicial Response

The liberalisation of locus standi has also led to misuse, with PILs being filed for oblique motives including harassment of opponents, seeking publicity, or delaying legitimate activities. The Supreme Court has in recent years imposed costs on frivolous PILs and emphasised the need for petitioners to demonstrate genuine public interest rather than personal or political agendas.

A writ issued by a court commanding a public official, government agency, or lower court to perform a duty imposed by law. The writ is issued when the authority has refused or failed to perform a duty that is ministerial in nature and is not discretionary.
From Latin meaning 'we command' — reflecting the directive nature of the writ from the court to the public authority.
A citizen files a mandamus petition when a government department fails to process their right-to-information application within the statutory time limit, compelling the department to act.

## Elements of Mandamus

For a writ of mandamus to lie, three elements must be established:
1. The petitioner has a legal right to the performance of the act.
2. The respondent has a corresponding legal duty to perform that act.
3. The respondent has failed or refused to perform that duty.

Mandamus lies only for ministerial duties — those that are mandatory and leave no discretion to the authority. It does not lie to compel the exercise of discretion in a particular way, though it can compel an authority to exercise its discretion.

## Mandamus Against Private Bodies

Traditionally, mandamus lay only against public authorities. However, Indian courts have progressively extended it to cover bodies that perform public functions, even if they are private entities. Universities, regulatory bodies, and entities exercising monopoly power over essential services have been brought within the ambit of mandamus through a purposive interpretation.

A legal doctrine that prevents the same parties from relitigating the same issues that have already been finally decided by a competent court. A matter once adjudicated cannot be reopened in subsequent proceedings between the same parties on the same facts.
From Latin meaning 'a matter already judged' or 'a thing decided' — capturing the principle of finality in judicial decisions.

A landlord files a second eviction suit against the same tenant on the same grounds after losing the first suit. The tenant raises res judicata as a defence, and the second suit is dismissed as barred by the earlier final judgment.

## Elements of Res Judicata Under the CPC

Section 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 codifies the doctrine of res judicata in India. For the doctrine to apply, the following conditions must be satisfied:
- The matter must have been directly and substantially in issue in the former suit.
- The former suit must have been between the same parties or parties claiming under them.
- The parties must have litigated under the same title.
- The court that decided the former suit must have been competent to try the subsequent suit.
- The matter must have been heard and finally decided.

## Constructive Res Judicata

An important extension of the doctrine is "constructive res judicata" — a point that might and ought to have been raised in the earlier proceedings, but was not, is deemed to have been decided against the party who did not raise it. This prevents piecemeal litigation where a party deliberately holds back arguments to reopen a case later.

## Res Judicata and Constitutional Courts

The doctrine applies in modified form to writ petitions before constitutional courts. While a habeas corpus petition can generally be filed repeatedly since liberty is at stake, other writ petitions are subject to res judicata principles. The Supreme Court has held that a second writ petition on the same cause of action, between the same parties, will generally be barred by constructive res judicata.

A petition seeking special leave (permission) to appeal to the Supreme Court of India against any judgment, decree, or order of any court or tribunal in India. Governed by Article 136 of the Constitution, it is a discretionary power of the Supreme Court to grant leave to appeal in cases involving substantial questions of law or gross miscarriage of justice.
Derived from the concept of 'leave' (permission) to appeal, with 'special' indicating the extraordinary and discretionary nature of the remedy under Article 136.

A party aggrieved by a High Court judgment in a criminal matter files an SLP before the Supreme Court, arguing that the High Court made a serious error of law in acquitting the accused despite clear evidence of guilt.

## Article 136 of the Constitution

Article 136 is among the most broadly worded provisions in the Indian Constitution. It gives the Supreme Court the power to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment, decree, determination, sentence, or order in any cause or matter passed or made by any court or tribunal in the territory of India. The only explicit exclusion is courts or tribunals constituted by or under any law relating to the Armed Forces.

## Discretionary Nature

The grant of special leave is entirely at the discretion of the Supreme Court. The court will not ordinarily interfere merely because it might have decided the matter differently. Leave is typically granted when:
- There is a substantial question of law of general importance.
- The case involves a question upon which the Supreme Court's pronouncement is needed.
- There has been a manifest miscarriage of justice.
- The lower court's approach is perverse or ignores binding precedent.

## The SLP as the Gateway

The SLP has become the primary gateway to the Supreme Court, with thousands filed every year. The court typically entertains around 60,000-70,000 SLPs annually and admits only a fraction for full hearing. The high volume of SLPs is itself a subject of debate — it contributes to the court's enormous docket burden and has led to calls for a reform of the Supreme Court into a constitutional court that hears only matters of constitutional importance.

A Latin phrase meaning 'on its own motion'. When a court takes cognisance of a matter without any formal petition being filed before it, it is said to act suo motu. This power allows courts to act on their own initiative in matters of public importance or grave injustice.
From Latin 'suo motu' meaning 'by its own motion' or 'on its own initiative' — as opposed to acting in response to a petition.
The Supreme Court takes suo motu cognisance of a newspaper report about the mistreatment of migrant workers and issues notice to the state government to respond to the allegations.

## Constitutional Basis

The suo motu power of Indian constitutional courts flows primarily from Articles 32 and 226 of the Constitution and has been read expansively by the courts themselves. The power allows courts to transcend the adversarial model of litigation and act in the public interest when they become aware of a constitutional violation through media reports, letters from individuals, or other sources.

## Controversy Around Suo Motu Powers

The exercise of suo motu powers has attracted both praise and criticism. Proponents argue that it allows courts to protect rights even when affected individuals lack the resources or knowledge to approach the court. Critics argue that it blurs the line between the judicial and executive functions, allowing courts to take on policy questions beyond their institutional competence. The extent to which suo motu actions can be effectively monitored and followed up is also a recurring concern.

## Notable Suo Motu Actions

Among the most significant suo motu actions of the Supreme Court have been: taking cognisance of the Bhopal gas tragedy; the right to food case during the 2002 drought; the Pegasus spyware case; and various matters relating to COVID-19 relief during the pandemic.
